original_abstract: The article constitutes an analysis of the final pericope of the Book of the Wisdom of Sirach (Sir 51:13-30) in its Greek version. The text can be divided into two parts: in the first one (vv. 13-22) the author relates his personal experience of seeking and finding wisdom, while in the second part (vv. 23-30) he admonishes those who lack education to join his school and partake of the wisdom he himself has found. The wisdom the sage refers to is of a religious character (he asked for it in prayer – vv. 13b, 14a, 19c; it manifests itself in good deeds – v. 19b). Not only does the author make it clear in the first part of the pericope that the vital requirement for finding wisdom is to start early in life (vv. 13a, 15d), but he also explains the ways in which wisdom may be found (a human being should subordinate all spheres of life to finding it – vv. 13b, 14b, 15b, 19a, 21a). The pericope lists the means necessary to find wisdom (namely, purification – v. 20b, following the path of righteousness – v. 15c, striving for goodness – v. 18a, turning to wisdom – v. 20a, complying with the Law – v. 19b, listening – v. 16a, and repentance for the mistakes made – v. 19d) as well as mentions the gifts wisdom grants those who find it (joy – v. 15b, formation – v. 16b, heart, that is intellectual capabilities – v. 20c, language thanks to which one can praise God and share wisdom with others – v. 22a; all of these gifts are called a good possession – v. 21b). The sage emphasizes the fact that finding wisdom is not particularly difficult and does not require a tremendous effort (v. 16a), but searching for wisdom should be a constant part of life for those who wish to have it (they should never stop searching for it – v. 14b). In part two (vv. 23-30), which is of a didactic character, the sage encourages the uneducated to join his school so that he can share wisdom with them. The author admonishes those who – like him – desire wisdom to take advantage of his experience and undergo formation in his school. Sirach presents two ways leading to wisdom. The first one requires personal involvement in searching for it, whilst the second one entails taking possession of wisdom from someone who has already found it. In Sir 51:13-30 the author expresses great optimism concerning the possibility of finding and possessing wisdom. His position is at odds with the pessimism of the Book of Job and the Book of Ecclesiastes in this respect. The pericope under analysis here serves as a summary of the opinions expressed by the sage earlier in his book (cf. especially 6:18-37; 14:20 – 15:10; 24, 33:18-19). Together with Sir 1:1-10 it the frames the whole book and constitutes the recapitulation of the most significant theological aspects of Sirach’s text as well as a synthesis of his theology.
humanized_abstract: The present article is an analysis of the Greek version of the last pericope of the Book of the Wisdom of Sirach, namely Sir 51:13-30. The entire text can be divided into two parts. In the first part (vv. 13-22) the author relates his own experience of the search for wisdom and its being found. In the second part (vv. 23-30) he exhorts the uneducated to come to his school and share in the wisdom he has found. The wisdom for which the sage asks and which he has found is of a religious nature (he asked for it in prayer – vv. 13b, 14a, 19c; it manifests itself in good deeds – v. 19b). In the first part of the pericope it is clearly stated that the basic condition for finding wisdom is that one begins to seek it from an early age (vv. 13a, 15d). It is also explained how wisdom can be found (a human being should subordinate all spheres of life to finding it – vv. 13b, 14b, 15b, 19a, 21a). The means of finding wisdom are also listed (purification – v. 20b; following the path of righteousness – v. Sir 51:13-30: An Analysis of the Greek Version The means of finding wisdom are also listed (purification – v. 20b; following the path of righteousness – v. 15c; striving for goodness – v. 18a; turning to wisdom – v. 20a; complying with the Law – v. 19b; listening – v. 16a; repentance for the mistakes made – v. 19d). In the pericope it is also mentioned what kind of gifts wisdom brings to those who have found it. The gifts of wisdom are joy (v. 15b), formation (v. 16b), heart (intellectual capabilities) (v. 20c) and language (through which it is possible to praise God and share wisdom with others) (v. 22a). The whole set of these gifts is called a good possession (v. 21b). The finding of wisdom is not depicted as being particularly difficult to achieve, nor as demanding great effort (v. 16a). What is important is that the seeking of wisdom should be a permanent feature of the life of those who want to have wisdom (they should never stop searching for it – v. 14b). In the second part of the pericope (vv. 23-30) of clearly didactic character, the author exhorts the uneducated to come to his school so that he could share wisdom with them. The author exhorts those who, like himself, desire wisdom to make use of his experience and to undergo the formation offered in his school. The Book of the Wisdom of Sirach presents two ways which lead to wisdom. The first way involves the personal participation of the individual who seeks wisdom, while the second way is that of taking possession of wisdom which has already been found and which is communicated by someone else. The author of the Book of the Wisdom of Sirach has clearly optimistic views on the possibility of finding and having wisdom. His attitude is different from that of the author of the Book of Job and the Book of Ecclesiastes. The present pericope may be seen as a summary of the author’s optimism as it is expounded in the Book of the Wisdom of Sirach. The author mentions his experience in searching for and finding wisdom in Sir 6:18-37; 14:20 – 15:10; 24, 33:18-19. Together with the prologue (Sir 1:1-10) it also frames the whole book and constitutes the summary of the most important theological ideas of the Book of the Wisdom of Sirach and at the same time a synthesis of the author’s theology.
